Chemicals A and B are considered inexhaustible, so if the first reaction creates X faster than the second reaction irreversibly destroys X ( k 2 b < k 1 a ), then the amount of X will grow unbounded. But if the first reaction creates X slower than the second reaction irreversibly destroys X ( k 1 a < k 2 b ), then the amount of X will approach 0. 3.3 Laser Threshold 3.3.1 n ˙ =   G n N   −   k n N ˙ =   − G n N   −   f N   +   p a) If N ˙   ≈   0 , then 0 ≈   − G n N   −   f N   +   p N ≈   p G n   +   f n ˙ =   G n N   −   k n   ≈   G p n G n   +   f   −   k n b) d n ˙ d n =   G p f ( G n   +   f ) 2   −   k d n ˙ d n | n * = 0 =   G p f f 2   −   k   =   G p f   −   k   >   0 ⇒   p   >   k f G p   =   p c c) If we set G = k = f = 1, n ˙   ≈   p m n   +   1   −   n   =   0   ⇒   n   =   0   or   n   =   p   −   1 A transcritical bifurcation occurs at p c . d) n ˙   =   G n N   −   k n   =   ( G N   −   k ) n N ˙   =   − G n N   −   f N   +   p   =   − ( G n   +   f )   N   +   p The assumption that N almost isn’t changing relative to n is valid if | GN – k | ≪ | Gn + f )|. Then N will initially change far more rapidly than n , but then stay put once it reaches an equilibrium with the current n value. Now n will still change, but N will reach the equilibrium with the newer n value almost instantly, keeping N ˙   ≈   0 . 3.4 Pitchfork Bifurcation 3.4.1 x ˙   =   r x   +   4 x 3 A subcritical pitchfork bifurcation occurs at r = 0. 3.4.3 x ˙   =   r x   −   4 x 3 A supercritical pitchfork bifurcation occurs at r = 0. 3.4.5 x ˙   =   r   −   3 x 2 A saddle-node bifurcation occurs at r = 0. 3.4.7 x ˙   =   5   −   r e − x 2 A saddle-node bifurcation occurs at r = 5. 3.4.9 x ˙   =   x   +   tanh ( r x ) A subcritical pitchfork bifurcation occurs at r = −1. 3.4.11 x ˙   =   r x   −   sin ( x ) a) There are an infinite number of fixed points when r = 0 at every integer multiple of π. All even multiples of π are stable, and all odd multiples of π are unstable. b) For r ≥ 1, there is only one intersection between y = rx and y = sin( x ), which is an unstable fixed point at the origin. c) There are no bifurcations until r ≤ 1, and the first bifurcation is a subcritical pitchfork bifurcation at r = 1. Note: To add more rigor to our bifurcation classification, we can expand in a Taylor series about x = 0, which gives x ˙   =   r x   −   sin ( x )   ≈   r x   −   ( x   −   x 3 3 !   +   x 5 5 !   −   ⋯ )   ≈   ( r   −   1 ) x   +   x 3 3 ! This is the normal form of a subcritical pitchfork bifurcation at r – 1 = 0. There are more bifurcations as r continues to decrease toward zero. However, all these subsequent bifurcations are saddle-node bifurcations. The first of these bifurcations occurs at r ≈ 0.1284 when the line y = rx becomes tangent to a hilltop of the sine wave.
